title: 2025/10/16 - PP
description: Thursday, 16 October 2025 | Primary Production Petition of Vegan Society of Aotearoa: Ensure accurate labelling of vegetarian and vegan products and Petition of NZ Vegetarian Society: Ensure Accurate Labelling of Vegetarian and Vegan Products Vegan Society Claire Insley, Media Spokesperson NZ Vegetarian Society Ina Babic, Editor
descriptionSummary: Primary Production. Petition of Vegan Society of Aotearoa: Ensure accurate labelling of vegetarian and vegan products and Petition of NZ Vegetarian Society: Ensure Accurate Labelling of Vegetarian and Vegan Products. Vegan Society

And we're just, um, wanting the same in New Zealand, essentially, because currently there is no actual legal meaning of vegan or vegetarian, which can be confusing for consumers. It's obviously also confusing for producers and manufacturers who may inadvertently say that something is vegan when actually isn't, because they don't actually know what actually is vegan.

And so we're requesting that, um, the, um, laws be up, um, upgraded, shall we say, to include those definitions. Um, so that, uh, everyone is protected. Everyone knows what is vegan, everyone knows what is vegetarian. Um, in our submission, we did, um, make a list of those things that we would like to be included.

And, and the, the, there's the risk that the vagaries of what is and what isn't will have a significant cost to the crown to sort of regulate that reality. What do you think? I'll come to you, Joe. It's, we just are seeking clarity in the law about what those words actually mean. Vegan should mean X, Y, z, vegetarian should mean X, y, Z. So that's, that's all that we're asking for. Um, as I said, I don't really feel that there would be unnecessary burden of cost to, to anybody.

Um, but it would mean that should in future people come across products that say they are vegan and then they actually contain milk powder, that we then have recourse to say, Hey, this has been incorrectly labeled. Um, you know, this, something needs to be done about this. Um, whereas currently we don't actually have that recourse.

Mm. Okay. Thank you, Claire, Steve, and then Joe Luxton has a question. Thank you, Steve. Lead Off. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Because there are people who are allergic to milk products to eggs, um, and therefore, if they see a product that is marked vegan and they think, oh, this is vegan, it will not have milk in it, it will not have eggs in it.

And they don't bother to go through those tiny, tiny writing of the ingredient list because they've accepted that the label that said vegan on the packaging was correct. And then they get it home and they go through and they say, oh, actually, okay, yes, it is in bold contains milk, but I didn't see that when I was buying it, and I relied on the vegan label.

Um, and so this, this is where we feel that there is a problem. Um, and so the, um, the UK has, has put that into law. Um, and so yeah, we, we feel that it's just going to help everybody, businesses, manufacturers, consumers, uh, ev everyone really.

Then, um, yeah, we what swap Thank you. Clear, clear. Steve's got a supplement on that. Yeah. Um, I believe that we based our definition on, on theirs, so, yes. Yeah. So it's not a lot of work for us in New Zealand. We could just do what the UK's done. Mm-hmm. Pretty much, yes, this is my feelings and, and a lot of foods are indeed correctly labeled, but there are also some which aren't, and this is, this is, and so this is where the problem is,

and we just want to be able to say, Hey, that's actually not correctly labeled. Can you please readjust your packaging and, and, and label things correctly? Brilliant. Thank you. Thank you, Claire. I've got a question from Hamus. Excellent. Thank you, Claire. And thank you for your petition. Uh, well, it should be. Yeah. Absolutely. It should be. And it shouldn't be on, on sale in the first place. So, so we actually already have legislation that would capture that example, But it doesn't because there have been foods, as in our, um, evidence that we supplied with the written submission, um, you'll see that there were a number of products listed in there that did have, um, non-vegan ingredients, but yet they were labeled as vegan.

So, But, but in effect, that product has breached the law, their existing laws, no. Um, so do, do we need more law or Do we actually just need to enforce a law that's already a available? I think we need clarity within the law because to my understanding, there is not an actual legal definition of vegans, and there is an actual legal definition of vegetarian.

And this is, this is the problem. And that's why, um, things can sort of either intentionally or unintentionally fall into the wrong category. Mm-hmm. Thank you, Hamish, and thank you Claire. Um, is there any final or further questions for Claire? We have her for a few more minutes, and then we'll, yeah, I need Steve. Oh, yeah.

If, if there's time. Yeah, just, um, clear it's a presumption and it sort of extends from, uh, Mr. Campbell's question, a presumption that the burden fall on the, uh, consumer to do the micro reading of all those ingredients. Um, what would be gained in the, you know, day-to-day practical effort of choosing to avoid, uh, dairy products, for example, if you're a vegan, if there was real certainty that there was a legal definition and that when you saw that label, you knew that it met that legal, legal definition that would take away some

of the stress and the burden for food buyers who were trying, who needed to avoid, uh, dairy products for health reasons, or wanted to for other reasons. AB absolutely. I mean, we do have a vegan certification scheme running in New Zealand, and, um, some manufacturers do go through that certification scheme, right?

And, and then, um, that obviously that does cost them money. Um, but that then is an absolute guarantee for anybody that if that vegan certification is on that product, that the, um, vegetarian society has indeed gone through and made sure that those ingredients are exactly what they say they are and therefore does meet the vegan criteria.

Um, that's why we want to put this more as a legal definition in law so that people don't all have to go through that certification process. Um, I mean, we have a number of somewhat confusing labels. You may have seen them already yourselves, um, vegan friendly. Now even vegans don't really know what that means.

Vegan friendly. What does that mean? It turns out what it means is that they haven't intentionally put in non-vegan ingredients, but that that product is manufactured in a plant which manufactures other non-graded, non-vegan, um, foods. Um, so it, it would stop these kind of things being there.

We we'd like, we'd like it to be just clear what is vegan friendly? No, don't tell us it's vegan friendly. Is it vegan or not? That's all we wanna know. Is there something in there that is, is going to cause harm to me? Sorry. Sorry. No further questions. We've got another submitter waiting. Yeah. Thank you, Claire. Exceedingly grateful for your time.

I think your submission was wholesome and fulsome, and we've learned a lot. Um, certainly people like me quite ignorant to a lot of this. So thank you very much, um, and have a wonderful day. Thank you. No worries. Thank you, Claire. Ina Hala, good to see you online. We've just been listening to Claire about issues to do with the vegan society.

Um, we're now going to move across to you good morning, uh, to discuss, uh, the Vegetarian society and what you think about the difficulties in terms of labeling. Great. Thank you, uh, for the opportunity to speak today. Uh, my name is Ina Babich and I'm here representing the New Zealand Vegetarian Society, a nonprofit organization that has been supporting vegetarians, vegans, and those transitioning to a more ethical, sustainable diet for over 80 years.

Um, our petition, uh, parliament, um, the parliament introduced legislation to ensure accurate labeling of vegetarian beam products reflecting a growing public demand for clarity, consistency, and fairness in how products are labeled, um, in author all. At present, there's no legal definition of vegetarian or vegan and New Zealand's labeling reg uh, regulations.

This means manufacturers can use these words or similar terms such as plant-based without meeting any consistent standard. As a result, consumers are being misled and ethical. Businesses that uphold genuine standards are being undercut by those that don't. For example, we've seen products labeled plant-based that still contain milk solids, confectionary marked vegan and vegetarian, that includes gelatin and snacks labeled suitable for vegans, despite containing egg and, uh, milk powder.

Each of these examples was found on New Zealand shelves in just the past three years. So this isn't simply a matter of preference or a lifestyle, it's a matter of consumer rights and fair trading. Um, when a label says vegan or vegetarian, people should be able to trust that information without having to study every ingredient or contact the manufacturer to check.

Um, this is also especially important for people with allergies, um, or those making choices based on religious beliefs. Um, the New Zealand Vegetarian Society is asking for clear, standardized, and enforceable definitions of these terms. Uh, written submission, um, which you, uh, already read, outlines a few practical steps, uh, standardizing the term plant-based.

So it can only be used for products that are a hundred percent plant derived, uh, banning vague claims such as less dairy, which have no defined meaning, um, and then defining vegan and vegetarian to reflect what consumers already understand, that vegan products contain no animal ingredients or byproducts at any stage, and that vegetarian products exclude meat, poultry, fish, and seafood with any animal derived additives, um, clearly disclosed.

So these definitions, um, also include practical safeguards such as measures to prevent cross-contamination, the use of GMO-free ingredients, and ensuring that no animal testing has been carried up, so that when a product carries these labels, consumers can rely on them completely. So these are straightforward common sense expectations that already align with how the public interprets these words.

Um, the Commerce Commission and its written response to the committee confirmed that the Fair Trading Act already prohibits misleading or deceptive conduct and allows for enforcement action where false or unsubstantiated claims are made. However, the act does not currently define what vegetarian or vegan mean, leaving these terms open to interpretation and potential misuse.

Um, introducing clear definitions within the Fair Trading Act would make enforcement more consistent and proactive, providing clarity for both consumers and for businesses. Importantly, the commission also noted that a review of the Fair Trading Act is planned and that this could provide an opportunity to strengthen protections by increasing penalties and improving deterrence against misleading labeling.

We believe this review offers the perfect opportunity to amend the Fair Trading Act to include clear and forcible definitions of terms such as vegetarian, um, and vegan. Other countries have already taken steps towards this. So the UK Food Standards Agency has issued formal guidance defining vegan and vegetarian labeling to protect consumers.

Uh, the European Union has worked toward harmonizing the definitions to prevent misleading claims, and its India goes even further legally defining vegetarian and non-vegetarian food and its food safety and standards regulations with clear symbols required on packaging. So these ex examples show that it can be done and the clear enforceable standards benefit both, uh, consumers and businesses.

The Museum Vegetarian Society does operate the vegetarian approved and vegan certified certification programs. These programs demonstrate that clear standards can work successfully in the marketplace, but voluntary certification alone can't replace consistent national definitions. So we're not asking for complex or costly reform only for clarity and fairness.

Consumers deserve to know what's in their products and the products they're buying. And ethical producers deserve a level playing field where, where misleading claims are not tolerated. With vegetarian vegetarianism and veganism becoming increasingly mainstream, the time has come to update the laws to reflect the choices New Zealanders make about what they buy and consume.

Um, and this would, you know, provide consumers, um, to protect them, reward integrity in business, and build trust in labels we can rely on. And that's, thank you for your time. Thank you very much.
